coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of carbon dots with multicolor emission for fluorescence detection of ascorbic acid, glutathione and moisture content
coredata.dc:creator: Liao, Linhong
Lin, Xiao
Zhang, Jie
Hu, Zhiyuan
Wu, Fengshou
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with multicolor emission (green, yellow, and red) were fabricated with naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride as carbon precursor, and different alkylamines as passive agents. The structures of the as-prepared carbon dots were confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectra, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. The characterized results and UV–vis absorption spectrum demonstrated that the luminescence variations of different CDs were probably attributed to the difference of N-related states, oxygen-containing groups, and oxidation degree in the CDs structure. Moreover, the green emitting CDs (G-CDs) could be utilized as fluorescent probe for ascorbic acid (AA) detection with detection limit (LOD) of 5 μM. The yellow emitting CDs (Y-CDs) could be used to detect glutathione (GSH) in the range of 1–70 μM with LOD of 0.07 μM. The red emitting CDs (R-CDs) could detect the water content in THF, DMF, acetone, and EtOH with LODs of 0.44%, 0.31%, 0.55% and 0.55%, respectively. Remarkably, G-CDs and Y-CDs can distinguish AA and GSH respectively with high selectivity and accuracy. Finally, the fluorescent probes based on G-CDs and Y-CDs were successfully applied for the AA and GSH determination in vitamin C tablet, grapefruit juice, and GSH whitening serum samples with high reliability and feasibility, which provided the guidance for the selection of AA and GSH supplements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multicolor emission
Fluorescence adjustment
AA detection
GSH detection
Moisture detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs
Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and AA
Fluorescent detection of Co2+ and GSH
Fluorescent detection of water content in organic solvents
Selective sensing of metal ions and biomolecules
Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and AA based on G-CDs
Fluorescent detection of Co2+ and GSH based on Y-CDs
Fluorescent detection of water content in organic solvents

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( NSFC ) (grant no. 21601142 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province ( 2021CFB228 , 2022CFC034 ). L. Liao acknowledges the financial support from the 14th Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant no. CX2022028 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images and size distributions of G-CDs (a, d); Y-CDs (b, e); and R-CDs (c, f).
(a) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs; (b) Raman spectra of N-CDs; (c) XRD patterns of N-CDs; (d) XPS survey of N-CDs.
High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of G-CDs (a–c); High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of Y-CDs (d–f); High-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s XPS spectra of R-CDs (g–i).

PL emission, PL excitation and UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs (a–c); PL emission spectrum of G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs under different excitations (d–f). Inset: photographs of corresponding G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs solution under the irradiation of UV lamp (365 nm).
PL intensity of the N-CDs solution with various pH values (a–c), NaCl concentrations (d–f), and storage time (g–i).

Fluorescence response of G-CDs solution (a), and Y-CDs solution (c) after the addition of different metal ions (1 mM); Fluorescence response of G-CDs + Fe3+ system (b), and Y-CDs + Co2+ system (d) upon the addition of various biomolecules (1 mM for G-CDs + Fe3+ system and 0.1 mM for Y-CDs + Co2+ system). The fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of metal ions and biomolecules was recorded as F0 and F, respectively.

(a) PL spectra of G-CDs upon addition of Fe3+ under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm; (b) Linear correlation between (F0–F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentrations (20–1200 μM); (c) PL spectra of G-CDs-Fe3+ system upon addition of AA under the excitation wavelength of 400 nm; (d) Linear correlation between (F–F0)/F0 and AA concentrations (40–700 μM). F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity before and after adding analyte, respectively.

(a) PL spectra of Y-CDs upon addition of Co2+ under the excitation wavelength of 470 nm; (b) Linear correlation between (F0–F)/F0 and Co2+ concentrations (1–50 μM); (c) PL spectra of Y-CDs-Co2+ system upon addition of GSH under the excitation wavelength of 470 nm; (d) Linear correlation between (F–F0)/F0 and GSH concentrations (1–70 μM). F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity before and after adding analyte, respectively.

Fluorescence spectra under the excitation wavelength of 580 nm with different water content of R-CDs in THF (a), DMF (b), Acetone (c), and EtOH (d), respectively; The linear correlation of F/F0 versus water content in THF (2%–25%) (e), DMF (2%–25%) (f), acetone (1%–50%) (g), and EtOH (5%–50%) (h), respectively.
Schematic illustration for the fabrication of N-CDs.
Comparison of LOD and linear range among the reported methods.
Detection of ascorbic acid based on G-CDs-Fe3+ system in real samples.
Detection of glutathione based on Y-CDs-Co2+ system in real sample.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of carbon dots with multicolor emission for fluorescence detection of ascorbic acid, glutathione and moisture content

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430205, PR China

Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry
School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei key Laboratory of Novel Reactor and Green Chemical Technology, Key Laboratory of Novel Biomass-Based Environmental and Energy Materials in Petroleum and Chemical Industry, School of Chemical Engineering and Pharmacy, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, 430205 P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Center for Nanoscience and Technology, Beijing, 100190, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Pharmaceutical and Chemical Engineering, Taizhou University, Jiaojiang, 318000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmaceutical and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Pharmaceutical and Chemical Engineering, Taizhou University, Jiaojiang, 318000, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with multicolor emission (green, yellow, and red) were fabricated with naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride as carbon precursor, and different alkylamines as passive agents. The structures of the as-prepared carbon dots were confirmed by the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Raman spectra, and X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. The characterized results and UV–vis absorption spectrum demonstrated that the luminescence variations of different CDs were probably attributed to the difference of N-related states, oxygen-containing groups, and oxidation degree in the CDs structure. Moreover, the green emitting CDs (G-CDs) could be utilized as fluorescent probe for ascorbic acid (AA) detection with detection limit (LOD) of 5 μM. The yellow emitting CDs (Y-CDs) could be used to detect glutathione (GSH) in the range of 1–70 μM with LOD of 0.07 μM. The red emitting CDs (R-CDs) could detect the water content in THF, DMF, acetone, and EtOH with LODs of 0.44%, 0.31%, 0.55% and 0.55%, respectively. Remarkably, G-CDs and Y-CDs can distinguish AA and GSH respectively with high selectivity and accuracy. Finally, the fluorescent probes based on G-CDs and Y-CDs were successfully applied for the AA and GSH determination in vitamin C tablet, grapefruit juice, and GSH whitening serum samples with high reliability and feasibility, which provided the guidance for the selection of AA and GSH supplements.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the most representative nanometer-sized materials [1], exhibit unique photophysical properties that can be modulated by the sp2 conjugated domains [2], surface chemical structures [3], and doping elements [4]. Because of their superior advantages including unique photoluminescence (PL), easy preparation and purification, adjustable optical property, and low cytotoxicity [5–8], CDs contribute to the advances in the fields of photocatalysis, drug delivery, bioimaging, light-emitting devices, and biosensors [9–12]. In recent years, the emission-tunable CDs are highly desired considering the investigation of the PL mechanisms and the preparation of CDs with desired optical properties. For examples, Liu et al. [13] studied the fluorescence effect of CDs that prepared from citric acid and four nitrogen-containing organic compounds. Sun et al. synthesized multicolor CDs by controlling graphitization and surface functionalization [14]. Lin et al. prepared a series of multicolor CDs using different carbon sources, and the PL redshift of CDs was attributed to the increased nitrogen content [15]. Although these studies successfully reported the CDs with tunable photoluminescence, the facile synthetic method for the fabrication of CDs with long-wavelength and multicolor emission is still highly desirable.

In general, the optical property of CDs was related with the synthesis conditions, carbon precursor [16], and heteroatoms doping [17], such as nitrogen, boron, or sulfur. Nitrogen doping (amino, amidic, pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic, and nitro) could adjust the energy gap, thus causing the redshift of wavelength [3,18–21]. Although the previous studies have demonstrated a strong correlation between the N-related states and PL behavior of CDs, the effect of nitrogen has not yet been clearly addressed. Besides, the selection of suitable carbon precursor is also very important for the successful fabrication of long-wavelength and multicolor emissive CDs. The precursor containing aromatic rings is conducive to the formation of large conjugated structures during the condensation and carbonization process, thus increasing the probability of long-wavelength emission [22,23]. Thus, it is desirable to fabricate multicolor CDs with long wavelength emission and investigate their potential fluorescence mechanism and applications.

Currently, carbon dots have been widely used for analytes detection due to the superiorities of easy operation, low cost, and high specificity and sensitivity [24–26]. Antioxidants, such as ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH), widely existing in plants, fruits and functional products, play significant role in providing antioxidation function, enhancing immunity, and preventing diseases [27–29]. Monitoring the AA/GSH level in human body and taking in AA/GSH from functional products or daily diets are of vital importance. Therefore, it's highly desirable to develop economic approaches for the selective detection of analytes such as AA and GSH. 1,4,5,8-Naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (NTCDA), is a kind of large conjugated carbon precursor containing two aromatic rings, which is the potential candidate to construct CDs with large conjugated carbon cores. However, due to their low solubility in most solvents, the fabrication of NTCDA-based CDs was rarely reported. In this work, we constructed three nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs), which displayed green, yellow, and red emission by using NTCDA as carbon source and ethylenediamine, polyethyleneimine or ethanolamine as nitrogen source, respectively (Scheme 1 ). Additionally, the green-emissive CDs (G-CDs) was utilized as a fluorescent “on-off-on” probe for the determination of Fe3+ and AA, the yellow-emissive CDs (Y-CDs) exhibited the high sensitivity and specificity for the determination of Co2+ and GSH, and the red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) could measure the moisture in organic solvents. Finally, the established sensing platforms were applied for the AA assay in daily diets, and GSH assay in skincare product with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Ethylenediamine, ethanolamine, polyethyleneimine (PEI), 1,4,5,8-naphthalenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (NTCDA), glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), arginine (Arg), histidine (His), valine (Val), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), alanine (Ala), phenylalanine (Phe), isoleucine (Ile), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), manganese chloride (MnCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3), ferrous chloride (FeCl2), lead (II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2), cerium trichloride (CeCl3), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), copper (II) sulfate (CuSO4), copper(I) chloride (CuCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), potassium bromide (KBr), potassium iodide (KI), calcium carbonate (CaCO3), stannous chloride (SnCl2) and cesium chloride (CsCl) were supplied by Aladdin. Other reagents were analytical grade, and ultrapure water was utilized in the whole experimental process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The multicolor N-CDs (G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs) were prepared by a solvothermal method using DMF as solvent. Specifically, 0.5 g of NTCDA and 0.3 g of nitrogen source (ethylenediamine, polyethyleneimine or ethanolamine) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF. The solution was then transferred into a 20-mL Teflon autoclave. After heated at 200 °C for 8 h, the resulting solution was purified by centrifuging (11,000 rpm) for 6 min to remove impurities and sediment, followed by dialysis (1000 Da) to remove residual chemicals. Finally, the powders of N-CDs were collected via freeze-drying under vacuum and the corresponding N-CDs could emit green, yellow, and red fluorescence, which were denoted as G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ and AA
ce:section.ce:para: For Fe3+ detection, 2 mL of DMF and 30 μL of the as-prepared G-CDs (1 mg/mL) were added into a 3 mL cuvette. The mixture was then stirred thoroughly and the fluorescence spectra was obtained under excitation of 420 nm. Subsequently, various concentrations of Fe3+ was added into the above system and the fluorescence spectra was re-measured. The PL variation in the absence and presence of Fe3+ was used to quantitatively analyze the concentration of Fe3+. For the detection of AA, various concentration of AA was added to the system of G-CDs + Fe3+, which contained 2 mL of DMF, 30 μL of G-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 100 μL of Fe3+ solution (30 mM). The enhancement of fluorescence intensity was recorded under excitation of 420 nm for the quantitative evaluation of AA. Similar procedures were followed for selectivity studies and real samples analysis.

The selectivity studies of G-CDs towards Fe3+ and AA were performed with various metal ions and biological molecules as interferences, including Mg2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Na+, Li+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Cu2+, Cs+, Ce3+, Co2+, Cu+, GSH, Lys, Ala, Val, Cys, Glu, Gly, Arg, Ile, Phe and His.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Co2+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: For Co2+ detection, 2 mL of DMF and 50 μL of Y-CDs (2 mg/mL) were added to a 3 mL cuvette. The mixture was then thoroughly stirred and the fluorescence spectra was obtained under excitation of 470 nm. Subsequently, various concentration of Co2+ was added into the above system and the fluorescence spectra was re-measured after homogeneous mixing. The PL variation in the absence and presence of Co2+ was used to quantitatively analyze Co2+. For the determination of GSH, different concentration of GSH was added to the Y-CDs + Co2+ system containing 2 mL of DMF, 50 μL of as-prepared Y-CDs solution (2 mg/mL) and 100 μL of Co2+ solution (10 mM). The enhancement of fluorescence intensity was recorded under excitation of 470 nm for quantitative evaluation of GSH. Similar procedures were followed for selectivity studies and real samples analysis.

The selectivity studies of Y-CDs towards Co2+ and GSH were performed with various metal ions and biological molecules as interferences, including Mg2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Cs+, Ce3+, AA, Lys, Ala, Val, Cys, Glu, Gly, Arg, Ile, Phe and His.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: DMF, THF, acetone, and EtOH were selected as model organic solvents for water content detection. Specifically, 50 μL of R-CDs (5 mg/mL) was added to DMF, THF, acetone, and EtOH with different proportions of water (vol%). The fluorescence intensity at 680 nm was then recorded under excitation of 590 nm.

Vitamin C tablet, grapefruit, and GSH whitening serum were selected as real samples for AA and GSH measurement in this study. Specifically, 1 g of Vitamin C tablet was added to 50 mL of water. The resulting solution was filtered through a 0.8 μm filter membrane and further diluted to an appropriate volume. In order to prepare grapefruit juice samples, the fruit was firstly washed and dried in the air, followed by squeezing in a mortar and filtrating with polyethersulfone membrane (PES) (0.8 μm) to obtain fresh fruit juice. Then, the obtained clear solution was diluted to the detection range with aqueous solution. To prepare GSH whitening serum sample, the solution was filtered through PES followed by the dilution to an appropriate volume. Subsequently, the above diluted samples were added to the corresponding G-CDs + Fe3+ or Y-CDs + Co2+ system similar to the standard detection procedures.

The multicolor N-CDs (G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs) were synthesized by one-step solvothermal approach using NTCDA as carbon source and ethylenediamine, polyethyleneimine or ethanolamine as nitrogen source (Scheme 1). The morphologies and structures of N-CDs were evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), Raman spectrometer, X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM images in Fig. 1 indicated that N-CDs were all uniformly dispersed, with average diameters about 7.3 nm, 2.4 nm and 6.5 nm for G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, respectively. Thus, the emission wavelengths of G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were not related to their particle size. In addition, the lattice spacing of G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were determined to be 0.26, 0.25, and 0.23 nm, respectively, as depicted in the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (inset in Fig. 1a–c), indicating all these N-CDs exhibited similar graphite-like core structures. The functional groups of the as-prepared N-CDs was analyzed through FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S1a, comparing with that of precursor (NTCDA), N-CDs displayed a new signal around at 3435 cm−1, corresponding to the N–H/O–H stretching vibration. Additionally, the new peak at 2923 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of –CH2. The peak at 1730 cm−1 was ascribed to the O–CO stretching vibration in anhydride vanished, while peak at 1650 cm−1 was attributed to stretching vibration of OC–N. The above results indicated that N-CDs were successfully fabricated with NTCDA and nitrogen source as precursors. In addition, R-CDs displayed more obvious peaks at 2778-2864 cm−1, belonging to the vibrations of aromatic C–H bonds [30], indicating a higher aromatic structure and surface oxidation degree compared with G-CDs and Y-CDs. The Raman spectra and XRD curve were further measured to investigate the crystallinity of the as-prepared G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the Raman spectra of N-CDs all exhibited two peaks at 1596 and 1360 cm−1, corresponding to graphite (G band) and disordered (D band) carbon structures, suggesting the co-existence of sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbon in N-CDs [31]. The ratios of ID/IG for different N-CDs were similar (0.84), indicating the sufficient graphitization of N-CDs. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of N-CDs (Fig. 2c) exhibited obvious broad peaks centered at 22° for Y-CDs and R-CDs, and sharp peak at 26.9° for G-CDs, revealing the amorphous structure of these N-CDs [32]. The full XPS spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 2d) showed three prominent peaks at 286, 400, and 532 eV, which corresponded to the elements C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, indicating the successful doping of N element in N-CDs.

The optical properties of the multicolor N-CDs were explored by XPS measurements, UV–vis absorption, and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and PL spectra. The high resolution XPS measurements were conducted to identify the chemical bonds and surface state of N-CDs. In the high resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a, d and 3g), the C 1s spectra displayed four peaks at 284.7, 286.3, 287.7 and 288.3 eV for G-CDs, 285, 286.3, 287.7 and 288.8 eV for Y-CDs, and 284.9, 286.6, 287.2 and 288.7 eV for R-CDs, which could be attributed to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and CO, respectively [33]. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3b, e and 3h) exhibited amide N (398.6 eV for G-CDs and 399.3 eV for Y-CDs) and N–H (400.6 eV for G-CDs and 400.3 eV for Y-CDs), while R-CDs included three characteristic peaks corresponding to amide N (399.4 eV), N–H (400.3 eV), and graphitic N (402.1 eV) [31]. The formation of graphite N in R-CDs (Fig. 3h) may reduce the bandgap, thus reducing the energy of the corresponding optical transition, and resulting in an obvious redshift [18], which were in a good agreement with the reported literatures that the presence of graphitic nitrogen leaded to the transition from blue to red fluorescence [18,34,35]. The high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. 3c, f and 3i) all exhibited two peaks at around 532 and 533 eV, ascribed to CO and C–O, respectively [36]. Moreover, the ratio of CO to C–O increased gradually with the fluorescence redshift of N-CDs, indicating the red-shift of emission was probably related to CO bonds on the surface of CDs, corresponding to the oxidation degree. Thus, the increase of oxygen-containing groups, surface oxidation degree, and N-related states led to the emission redshift of CDs.

The absorption spectra in Fig. 4 a–c and Fig. S1b showed that NTCDA exhibited two main absorption peaks at 266 and 350 nm, probably originating from the π-π∗ electronic transition of sp2 aromatic benzene rings and n-π∗ transition of anhydride. In addition to absorption in the ultraviolet region, N-CDs exhibited obvious absorption bands ranging from 400 to 700 nm, indicating the larger conjugated sp2 domains and abundant surface functional groups compared to that of precursor (Fig. S2 and Table S1). Furthermore, the maximum excitation/emission wavelengths of G-CDs and Y-CDs were located at 396 nm/508 nm and 467 nm/546 nm, while R-CDs displayed two emission wavelengths at 620 nm and 680 nm with the corresponding excitation peaks at 542 nm and 583 nm (Fig. 4a–c). G-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs exhibited green, yellow and red fluorescence in DMF under excitation of UV lamp (365 nm). As shown in Fig. 4d–f, N-CDs exhibited the typical excitation-dependent emission behavior, probably originating from the diverse surface defects and functional groups [37]. Using rhodamine 6G and rhodamine B as the references, the PLQYs of G-CDs, Y-CDs and R-CDs in DMF solution were calculated to be 5.5%, 3% and 2%, respectively. Meanwhile, the emission spectra of N-CDs in various solvents including dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetone, ethanol, and water were carried out. As indicated in Fig. S3 and Table S2, strong fluorescence of N-CDs was observed in organic solvents, while relatively weak emission peaks were found in water, probably due to their poor solubility in water. Moreover, N-CDs exhibited the typical concentration-dependent emission property with the maximum PL intensity at 0.02 for G-CDs, 0.12 for Y-CDs and 0.24 mg/mL for R-CDs, respectively (Fig. S4).

The fluorescence stabilities of N-CDs were evaluated over a range of pH values (3–8), NaCl concentrations (0.1–0.5 mol/L), and storage times (0–18 days). As shown in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at pH = 5 for G-CDs, while the intensity decreased with the pH value (2–8) for Y-CDs and R-CDs, implying that the pH variation might cause the electronic transition changes of n-π∗, thus leading to the fluorescence decrease under different conditions. In addition, as the NaCl concentration was increased from 0.1 to 0.5 mol/L and the storage time extended to 18 days, the PL intensity did not exhibit any obvious reduction, indicating the high fluorescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective sensing of metal ions and biomolecules
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the nitrogen-rich CDs are known to interact with metal ions, thus resulting in fluorescence quenching via charge transfer mechanisms [38]. To investigate the potential of the as-prepared G-CDs and Y-CDs as fluorescent probes, their selectivity towards metal ions were evaluated. As displayed in Fig. 6 a, the fluorescence quenching effect of various metal ions, including Fe3+, Mn2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+, Na+, Li+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Cu2+, Cs+, Ce3+, Co2+, and Cu+ were measured. Among them, only Fe3+ could apparently quench the fluorescence of G-CDs, while the addition of other metal ions did not exhibit any obvious quenching effect. Furthermore, the selectivity of G-CDs-Fe3+ system towards various biomolecules, including AA, GSH, Lys, Ala, Val, Cys, Glu, Gly, Arg, Ile, Phe and His were investigated by comparing the recovery efficiency of PL. As shown in Fig. 6b, AA could recover the PL intensity of G-CDs-Fe3+ system with high selectivity. Similarly, the Y-CDs could be utilized as a fluorescent probe for Co2+ and GSH determination (Fig. 6c and d), while little effect of other interfering substances on the detection of GSH was observed, indicating the high selectivity. Remarkably, the as-prepared G-CDs and Y-CDs could accurately distinguish AA and GSH, while most of other reported methods exhibited poor selectivity for the detection of antioxidants including AA, GSH and Cys [28,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of Fe3+ and AA based on G-CDs
Fluorescent detection of Co2+ and GSH based on Y-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence reduction of Y-CDs towards Co2+ suggested that it was possible to apply Y-CDs for Co2+ sensing. As displayed in Fig. 8 a and b, the PL intensities of Y-CDs at 550 nm were obviously decreased with the concentration of Co2+, followed by the enhancement upon the addition of GSH. Moreover, the PL intensity decreased linearly with the concentration of Co2+ from 1 to 50 μM. The linear correlation between fluorescence change ((F0–F)/F0) and Co2+ concentration was described as (F0–F)/F0 = 0.01869 [Co2+] + 0.0657 from 0 to 10 μM, and (F0–F)/F0 = 0.00306 [Co2+] + 0.1712 from 10 to 50 μM. Based on this equation, the LOD of Y-CDs towards Co2+ was calculated to be 0.06 μM. Since GSH could specifically recover the PL intensity of Y-CDs-Co2+ mixture (Fig. 8c), the linear correlation between ((F–F0)/F0) and GSH concentration was measured and described as (F–F0)/F0 = 0.0161 [GSH] + 0.05187 in the low concentration range, with the LOD of 0.07 μM (Fig. 8d). Besides, a comparison of detection results of GSH was conducted among the reported materials in terms of LOD and linear range (Table 1). Thus, the as-prepared Y-CDs can be utilized as “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor for simultaneous determination of Co2+ and GSH with high selectivity.

In general, the fluorescence quenching can be explained by different mechanisms, such as dynamic quenching effect (DQE), resonance energy transfer (RET), static quenching effect (SQE), inner filter effect (IFE), and so on. According to the reported literatures, DQE and RET usually result in a significant reduction in fluorescence lifetime, while SQE and IFE do not. Additionally, RET and IFE require the spectrum overlap between CDs and quencher. For Fe3+ and Co2+, there is no obvious absorption peak in the emission range of 400–600 nm. Therefore, the quenching of the G-CDs and Y-CDs are not related to RET and IFE. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs and Y-CDs were calculated to be 4.5 and 5.85 ns, and changed to 4.03 and 5.82 ns after adding Fe3+ and Co2+, respectively. In addition, the Stern-Volmer equation was further performed with good linear behavior.
F 0
F
= 1 +
K SV
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]

The Stern-Volmer analysis of the quenching experiments (F0/F versus [Q]) was performed at 298 K. In this case, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+ or Co2+, τ0 is the lifetime of G-CDs or Y-CDs, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities before and after addition of Fe3+ or Co2+, respectively. Fitting of the quenching data to the above equation yielded a Kq of 4.3 × 1011 L/mol·s for G-CDs-Fe3+ system and Kq of 6.5 × 1012 L/mol·s for Y-CDs-Co2+ system, which are far greater than the maximum diffusion-rate constant (2.0 × 1010 L/mol·s), suggesting that the quenching of the G-CDs and Y-CDs are both initiated by static quenching. For the possible detection mechanisms of AA and GSH, Fig. 6a have confirmed that Fe3+ could quench the fluorescence of the G-CDs and Fe2+ could not. Due to the strong reducing property, AA can easily reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, which may lead to the fluorescence recovery of Y-CDs and enable the detection of AA. Additionally, GSH containing various functional groups (-NH2, –SH, –COOH, –OH) were prone to chelating with Co2+, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of Y-CDs and realizing GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The quantitative and qualitative detection of water in organic solvents is of vital importance in organic synthesis, especially in anhydrous reactions. Based on the significantly different PL property of R-CDs in organic solvents and water, the capability of R-CDs as fluorescent probes for water determination in THF, DMF, acetone, and EtOH was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 9 a-d, the fluorescent intensity of R-CDs in four kinds of solvents decreased gradually with the increase of water content. The fluorescence ratio (F/F0) is linearly correlated with water content in the organic solvents (Fig. 9e-h), where F0 and F denoted the PL intensity in the absence and presence of water, respectively. Taking water content in THF as an instance (Fig. 9e), the linear equation was described as F/F0 = −1.355x + 0.944 and the LOD (LOD = 3σ/K) was determined to be 0.44%. Besides THF, the water content in DMF, Acetone, and EtOH could be also determined, and the corresponding LOD was valued as 0.31%, 0.55% and 0.55%, respectively. Thus, it was feasible to employed R-CDs as fluorescent probes to quantitatively detect the moisture content in organic solvents without sophisticated processes.

To verify the feasibility of G-CDs-Fe3+ and Y-CDs-Co2+ system in practical application, different real samples including vitamin C (AA) tablets, grapefruit juice and GSH whitening serum samples were selected for the actual content detection. Specifically, a standard addition method was applied to evaluate the AA concentration in vitamin C tablet and Grapefruit juice samples, as well as GSH in GSH whitening serum sample. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries in different system were ranged from 98.6% to 105.5% with relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 5% (n = 3), suggesting that the G-CDs-Fe3+ system could be utilized as a sensing platform for the analysis of AA in actual samples. The results in Table 3 indicated the recoveries of GSH were obtained in the range of 100.3%–102.3% with RSD between 2.1% and 4%, indicating the great potential of Y-CDs-Co2+ system for GSH detection in real samples. Thus, G-CDs-Fe3+ and Y-CDs-Co2+could be used as efficient sensing platforms for the detection of AA and GSH respectively.

In summary, we developed three emission-adjustable N-doped CDs via solvothermal treatment of NTCDA and ethylenediamine, polyethyleneimine or ethanolamine. Remarkably, the same carbon precursor with different nitrogen-containing sources resulted in three N-CDs with controllable fluorescence at green, yellow, and red wavelength. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL property. The detailed characterizations further indicated that the different N-related states, oxygen-containing groups, and surface oxidation degree of N-CDs induced the different PL properties. Moreover, the PL of G-CDs could be quenched by Fe3+, and the addition of AA into the G-CDs/Fe3+ system could restore the PL. Based on this phenomenon, G-CDs could be applied as the fluorescent probe for selective determination of Fe3+ and AA with the detection limits of 2.4 and 5 μM, respectively. Similarly, Y-CDs could be utilized as fluorescent probe to detect Co2+ and GSH with LODs of 0.06 and 0.07 μM, respectively. In addition, R-CDs was employed as fluorescent probes to quantitatively detect the moisture content in organic solvents without sophisticated processes. Finally, the practical applications of G-CDs and R-CDs in measurement of AA and GSH in real samples were successfully achieved with satisfactory recoveries.

Linhong Liao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Xiao Lin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Jie Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Zhiyuan Hu: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Fengshou Wu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing -review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (grant no. 21601142), Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2021CFB228, 2022CFC034). L. Liao acknowledges the financial support from the 14th Graduate Innovative Fund of Wuhan Institute of Technology (grant no. CX2022028).
